metadata.notes: Risk classifications and article references are based on the final published text of the EU AI Act. Enforcement dates reflect the phased implementation schedule.

risk_tiers.label: Unacceptable Risk
risk_tiers.description: AI practices that pose a clear threat to fundamental rights, safety, or democratic values. These are outright prohibited under the EU AI Act.

risk_tiers.label: High Risk
risk_tiers.description: AI systems that pose significant risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights. Permitted but subject to strict obligations before market placement.

risk_tiers.label: Limited Risk
risk_tiers.description: AI systems with specific transparency obligations. Users must be informed they are interacting with AI.

risk_tiers.label: Minimal Risk
risk_tiers.description: AI systems with little to no risk to rights or safety. No mandatory obligations under the Act, though voluntary codes of conduct are encouraged.

use_cases.label: Real-Time Remote Biometric Identification in Public Spaces
use_cases.description: AI systems that identify individuals in real time using biometric data (e.g. facial recognition) in publicly accessible spaces. Prohibited with narrow exceptions for law enforcement (missing persons, terrorist threats) subject to prior judicial or independent authorisation.
real_world_examples: Live CCTV facial recognition in train stations or city centres
Automated crowd identification at public events
use_cases.prohibited_reason: Constitutes mass surveillance incompatible with EU fundamental rights principles.
obligations: Prohibited — no compliance pathway for general commercial use
exceptions: Prosecution of serious criminal offences — requires prior judicial authorisation

use_cases.label: Social Scoring by Public Authorities
use_cases.description: AI systems used by public authorities to evaluate and score individuals based on social behaviour or personal characteristics, leading to detrimental treatment in unrelated contexts.
real_world_examples: Social credit scoring systems used to restrict travel or public services
Aggregated citizen behavioural scoring systems
use_cases.prohibited_reason: Undermines human dignity, equality, and the rule of law.

use_cases.label: Subliminal Manipulation Techniques
use_cases.description: AI systems that deploy subliminal, manipulative, or deceptive techniques to distort a person's behaviour in ways that impair their ability to make free and informed decisions, causing harm.
real_world_examples: AI systems exploiting psychological vulnerabilities to drive compulsive purchasing
Micro-targeted political disinformation designed to exploit cognitive biases
use_cases.prohibited_reason: Undermines individual autonomy and informed decision-making.

use_cases.label: AI-Based Emotion Recognition in Workplaces and Schools
use_cases.description: AI systems that infer or classify emotions of natural persons in workplace or educational settings through biometric data such as facial expressions, voice, or physiological signals.
real_world_examples: Automated employee mood monitoring via camera feeds
Student attention and engagement scoring via facial recognition in classrooms
use_cases.prohibited_reason: Infringes on dignity and privacy in coercive settings where consent is structurally compromised.
exceptions: Medical or safety purposes (e.g. detecting drowsiness in vehicle operators)

use_cases.label: AI for Predictive Policing Based on Profiling
use_cases.description: AI systems used by law enforcement to predict criminal activity based solely on profiling of individuals or groups, without objective evidence or factual basis for suspicion.
real_world_examples: Risk-scoring software predicting criminal recidivism based on demographic data
Individual pre-crime prediction models used to pre-emptively detain or target suspects
use_cases.prohibited_reason: Violates presumption of innocence and risks systematic discrimination.
obligations: Prohibited for individual prediction — area-based risk assessment is separately evaluated

use_cases.label: AI-Assisted Medical Diagnosis and Triage
use_cases.description: AI systems used to support clinical decision-making, including diagnosis of disease, imaging interpretation, treatment recommendations, and patient triage. Classified as high-risk due to direct impact on patient health and safety.
real_world_examples: AI analysing MRI scans for tumour detection
Automated sepsis early-warning systems in hospitals
AI triage tools in emergency departments
key_obligations: High-quality training, validation, and test data (Article 10)

use_cases.label: Automated CV Screening and Recruitment Tools
use_cases.description: AI systems used to filter, rank, or score job applicants, including tools that analyse CVs, video interviews, or psychometric tests to determine candidate suitability.
real_world_examples: Automated applicant tracking systems with AI-driven shortlisting
AI video interview analysis scoring candidates on speech patterns or facial expressions
Résumé ranking tools trained on historical hiring decisions

use_cases.label: AI in Creditworthiness Assessment
use_cases.description: AI systems used by financial institutions to evaluate the creditworthiness of individuals, determine credit limits, or assess repayment risk — with direct impact on access to financial services.
real_world_examples: Machine learning models used by banks to approve or reject loan applications
AI-driven mortgage eligibility scoring
Buy-now-pay-later platform credit risk models
key_obligations: Risk management and data quality standards

use_cases.label: AI Systems in Criminal Justice (Sentencing and Risk Assessment)
use_cases.description: AI systems used to assist or inform judicial decisions including recidivism risk scoring, bail assessments, sentencing recommendations, and case prioritisation.
real_world_examples: Recidivism prediction tools used in pre-trial or sentencing hearings
Automated bail decision support systems
AI case outcome prediction for prosecutors or defence teams
key_obligations: Strict human oversight — AI is advisory only

use_cases.label: AI in Educational Assessment and Monitoring
use_cases.description: AI systems that assess learning outcomes, determine access to educational institutions, or monitor student performance in ways that influence educational trajectories.
real_world_examples: Automated essay grading systems used in university admissions
AI-based standardised test proctoring with behavioural monitoring
Adaptive learning platforms that gatekeep curriculum access based on performance

use_cases.label: AI in Critical Infrastructure Management
use_cases.short_label: AI for power / water / transport infrastructure
use_cases.description: AI systems that manage or make decisions in the operation of critical infrastructure including electricity grids, water systems, transport networks, and digital infrastructure.
real_world_examples: AI managing load balancing on national electricity grids
Automated fault detection systems for water distribution networks
AI-controlled rail switching systems

use_cases.label: AI-Powered Chatbots and Virtual Assistants
use_cases.description: AI systems deployed as conversational agents — including customer service bots, virtual assistants, and AI companions — where users may not immediately recognise they are interacting with an AI system.
real_world_examples: Customer service chatbots on e-commerce platforms
AI virtual assistants integrated into banking apps
General-purpose consumer chatbots
key_obligations: Mandatory disclosure that the user is interacting with an AI
Exception applies when context makes it obvious (e.g. clearly labelled entertainment bots)
exceptions: Context makes AI nature obvious to a reasonably informed user

use_cases.label: AI-Generated Deepfakes and Synthetic Media
use_cases.description: AI systems that generate or manipulate audio, image, or video content (deepfakes) that depicts real persons, places, or events in a way that could mislead the public.
real_world_examples: AI-generated video of public figures saying things they did not say
Synthetic news footage created without disclosure
AI face-swap tools used in online content
key_obligations: Machine-readable disclosure that content is AI-generated
Exception for clearly artistic or satirical content with appropriate labelling
exceptions: Clearly artistic, creative, or satirical work with disclosure

use_cases.label: General-Purpose AI Systems (GPAIs) and Foundation Models
use_cases.description: Large-scale AI models trained on broad data and deployed across a wide range of tasks and applications. Subject to specific transparency and documentation obligations under the EU AI Act, with additional obligations for systemic-risk models.
real_world_examples: Large language models such as GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini
Multimodal foundation models
Open-source models above the 10^25 FLOP training threshold
key_obligations: Technical documentation of model capabilities and limitations
Additional obligations for systemic-risk models (Article 55): adversarial testing, incident reporting, cybersecurity measures

use_cases.label: AI Spam Filters and Content Moderation
use_cases.description: AI systems used to detect and filter spam, classify content, or moderate user-generated material on digital platforms. Pose minimal risk under the EU AI Act.
real_world_examples: Email spam detection models
Social media automated content policy enforcement
Comment toxicity classifiers
key_obligations: No mandatory obligations under the Act
Voluntary adherence to AI codes of conduct encouraged

use_cases.label: AI in Video Games and Entertainment Recommendation
use_cases.description: AI systems used to generate game content, control non-player characters, or personalise entertainment recommendations on streaming platforms. Generally pose minimal risk.
real_world_examples: Procedural content generation in video games
AI-controlled NPCs with adaptive behaviour
Streaming platform recommendation engines (film, music, podcasts)
key_obligations: No mandatory obligations under the Act
General consumer protection and data protection laws still apply

use_cases.label: AI Writing and Productivity Assistants
use_cases.description: AI-powered tools that assist with writing, summarisation, translation, code completion, or task management in professional and consumer contexts.
real_world_examples: AI grammar and writing assistants
Code autocompletion tools
AI document summarisation tools
AI translation software
key_obligations: No mandatory obligations under the Act
May be subject to transparency obligations if embedded in a chatbot interface (Article 50)

use_cases.label: AI-Driven Border Control and Visa Assessment
use_cases.description: AI systems used by border authorities to assess migration and asylum applications, verify identities at borders, or classify traveller risk profiles.
real_world_examples: Automated lie-detection tools used at border crossings
AI risk-scoring of visa applications
Biometric identity verification at automated passport gates
key_obligations: Mandatory human oversight of all consequential decisions

use_cases.label: AI Robotic Surgery and Autonomous Medical Devices
use_cases.description: AI-driven robotic systems that assist or autonomously perform surgical procedures, or AI embedded in medical devices that influence diagnosis or treatment without constant human input.
real_world_examples: AI-assisted laparoscopic surgical robots
Autonomous insulin delivery systems
AI-guided radiotherapy dose planning
key_obligations: Conformity assessment under existing medical device regulation and EU AI Act
Extensive validation and clinical trials data
Human override capability at all stages

use_cases.label: AI Personalised Advertising and Behavioural Targeting
use_cases.description: AI systems that analyse user behaviour, preferences, and demographic signals to deliver personalised advertising. Governed primarily by existing GDPR and Digital Services Act obligations rather than specific EU AI Act requirements.
real_world_examples: Programmatic advertising platforms with AI bidding and targeting
Social media ad personalisation engines
Retargeting systems based on browsing history
key_obligations: No specific obligations under the EU AI Act
Subject to GDPR, Digital Services Act, and Digital Markets Act

implementation_timeline.label: Act enters into force
implementation_timeline.description: The EU AI Act officially enters into force across all EU member states.

implementation_timeline.label: Prohibited practices ban
implementation_timeline.description: Prohibitions on unacceptable-risk AI practices become enforceable (Article 5). Six months after entry into force.

implementation_timeline.label: GPAI model obligations
implementation_timeline.description: Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models (foundation models) apply. Twelve months after entry into force.

implementation_timeline.label: High-risk obligations
implementation_timeline.description: Full obligations for high-risk AI systems (Annex III) and limited-risk transparency obligations come into effect. Twenty-four months after entry into force.

implementation_timeline.label: High-risk embedded systems
implementation_timeline.description: Obligations for high-risk AI systems embedded in regulated products (Annex I — medical devices, machinery, aviation, etc.) become enforceable. Thirty-six months after entry into force.
